stem: The term 'schizophrenia' was coined in 1908 by which psychiatrist, and from which language and words was it derived?
options: Coined by Emil Kraepelin in 1896; derived from the Latin 'schizo' (divided) and 'phren' (brain)
Coined by Eugen Bleuler in 1908; derived from the Greek 'skhizo' (split) and 'phren' (mind)
Coined by Eugen Bleuler in 1911; derived from the Greek 'skhizo' (split) and 'psyche' (soul)
Coined by Sigmund Freud in 1908; derived from the Greek 'skhizo' (break) and 'phren' (mind)

stem: Which statement MOST precisely reflects what schizophrenia is probably responsible for compared to all other mental illnesses?
options: Schizophrenia is probably responsible for the highest rates of completed suicide, greatest medication non-compliance, and most exorbitant research costs
Schizophrenia is probably responsible for lengthier hospitalisations, greater chaos in family life, more exorbitant costs to individuals and governments, and more fears than any other mental illness
Schizophrenia is probably responsible for the most exorbitant treatment costs, greatest family burden, highest relapse rates, and most stigma of any chronic psychiatric disorder
Schizophrenia is probably responsible for more hospitalisations, greater individual suffering, more governmental concern, and more research than any other neurological disorder

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is asked about the suicide risk associated with schizophrenia. Which statistics are MOST precisely stated in the notes?
options: About one-quarter of people with schizophrenia attempt suicide and about 1 in 20 die from the act
About one-third of people with schizophrenia attempt suicide and about 1 in 10 die from the act
About one-half of people with schizophrenia attempt suicide and about 1 in 20 die from the act
About one-third of people with schizophrenia attempt suicide and about 1 in 15 die from the act

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q3): The nurse explains when schizophrenia most devastatingly strikes people. Which statement is MOST precisely grounded in the notes?
options: Schizophrenia strikes people just when they are preparing to enter the phase of their lives for highest growth and productivity â typically in the teens or early 20s â leaving most unable to return to normal young adult lives
Schizophrenia strikes people most severely in middle adulthood â typically in the 30s or 40s â leaving most unable to maintain employment, relationships, and financial independence
Schizophrenia strikes people during early childhood â typically before age 12 â leaving most unable to complete compulsory education and develop peer relationships
Schizophrenia strikes people at any age but is most devastating when onset occurs after 40, leaving most unable to maintain pre-morbid functioning acquired over a lifetime

stem: The four phases of the pattern of development of schizophrenia must be listed in the correct sequence. Which is MOST accurately ordered?
options: The prodromal phase, the pre-morbid phase, the active psychotic phase, and the residual phase
The pre-morbid phase, the prodromal phase, the active psychotic phase (schizophrenia), and the residual phase
The pre-morbid phase, the active psychotic phase, the prodromal phase, and the residual phase
The prodromal phase, the active psychotic phase, the residual phase, and the pre-morbid phase

stem: The pre-morbid personality in schizophrenia is described in the notes. Which characteristics are MOST completely and precisely listed?
options: Social maladjustment, social withdrawal, irritability, and antagonistic thoughts and behaviour
Social isolation, grandiosity, emotional lability, and aggressive behaviour
Social withdrawal, low self-esteem, depressed mood, and disorganised thinking
Social maladjustment, social withdrawal, suspiciousness, and passivity

stem: CASE STUDY: A psychiatric nurse is reviewing the history of a 19-year-old male now presenting with psychosis. His school records show he was very shy, had poor peer relationships, did poorly academically, and showed antisocial behaviour. Pre-morbid history also reveals what type of personality?
options: Histrionic or narcissistic personalities characterised as dramatic, attention-seeking, and grandiose
Schizoid or schizotypal personalities characterised as quiet, passive, and introverted with few friends
Antisocial or borderline personalities characterised as impulsive, manipulative, and emotionally unstable
Paranoid or avoidant personalities characterised as suspicious, guarded, and fearful of rejection

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q7): The nurse notes the pre-schizophrenic adolescent behaviour described in the notes. Which pattern is MOST precisely stated?
options: Pre-schizophrenic adolescents may have no close friend and no date, may avoid team sports, and may enjoy solitary activities to the exclusion of social activities
Pre-schizophrenic adolescents typically demonstrate academic excellence but social isolation, preferring structured activities and avoiding unstructured social interactions
Pre-schizophrenic adolescents show marked academic decline, engage in risk-taking behaviours, and preferentially seek online social connections over face-to-face interaction
Pre-schizophrenic adolescents typically show mood instability, school refusal, and intense peer conflict alongside solitary withdrawal from family activities

stem: The prodromal phase of schizophrenia is precisely defined in the notes. Which definition is MOST accurate?
options: The prodromal phase refers to the period of active psychotic symptoms before treatment is initiated; it begins when frank psychotic symptoms first appear and ends when antipsychotic medication is started

The prodromal phase refers to certain signs and symptoms that precede the characteristic manifestations of the acute, fully developed illness; it begins with a change from pre-morbid functioning and extends until the onset of frank psychotic symptoms

The prodromal phase refers to the period after recovery from a first psychotic episode during which vulnerability to relapse is highest; it begins after hospital discharge and extends until the patient achieves stable remission
The prodromal phase refers to the residual symptoms that persist after an acute psychotic episode; it begins after active psychosis resolves and extends until full pre-morbid functioning is restored

stem: What is the average length of the prodromal phase according to the notes?
options: Between 1 and 3 years
Between 2 and 5 years
Between 3 and 7 years
Between 6 months and 2 years

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is reviewing which symptoms appear during the prodromal phase. Which list is MOST completely and accurately derived from the notes?
options: Substantial functional impairment, sleep disturbance, anxiety, irritability, depressed mood, poor concentration, fatigue, and behavioural deficits such as deterioration in role functioning and social withdrawal; positive symptoms such as perceptual abnormalities, ideas of reference, and suspiciousness develop late in the prodromal phase
Auditory hallucinations, command delusions, disorganised speech, and grossly disorganised behaviour â all active psychotic symptoms that define the transition from prodrome to acute phase
Flat affect, alogia, avolition, and social isolation â purely negative symptoms that characterise the entire prodromal phase without any positive symptom precursors
Sleep disturbance, anxiety, and irritability only â positive symptoms do not appear until the frank psychotic phase is established

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q11): The nurse identifies what the late appearance of positive symptoms in the prodromal phase specifically heralds. Which statement is MOST precisely stated?
options: Late positive symptoms herald the transition from active psychosis to the residual phase of schizophrenia
Late positive symptoms such as perceptual abnormalities, ideas of reference, and suspiciousness herald the imminent onset of psychosis
Late positive symptoms herald the beginning of the pre-morbid phase and indicate genetic vulnerability to schizophrenia spectrum disorder
Late positive symptoms herald response to antipsychotic treatment and indicate that the prodromal phase is successfully resolving

stem: According to DSM-5 Criterion A for schizophrenia, how many of the listed symptoms must be present, for what time period, and which symptoms MUST be included among those present?
options: Three or more symptoms present for at least 2 weeks; at least one must be delusions, hallucinations, or disorganised speech
Two or more symptoms present for a significant portion of time during a 1-month period; at least one must be delusions (1), hallucinations (2), or disorganised speech (3)
Two or more symptoms present for at least 6 months; at least one must be delusions, hallucinations, or disorganised behaviour
Four or more symptoms present for a significant portion of time during a 2-month period; at least two must be delusions (1), hallucinations (2), or negative symptoms (5)

stem: All five symptoms listed under DSM-5 Criterion A must be identified. Which combination is MOST precisely and completely correct?
options: Delusions; hallucinations; disorganised speech; grossly disorganised or catatonic behaviour; negative symptoms (diminished emotional expression or avolition)
Delusions; hallucinations; disorganised speech; disorganised behaviour; flat affect and alogia
Delusions; hallucinations; disorganised thinking; catatonic behaviour; anhedonia and social withdrawal
Delusions; command hallucinations; disorganised speech; grossly disorganised behaviour; avolition and asociality

stem: CASE STUDY: A 22-year-old woman presents with auditory hallucinations and disorganised speech for 4 weeks. She has been functioning poorly at work for 8 months. DSM-5 Criterion B is being assessed. What does Criterion B MOST precisely require?
options: Criterion B requires that continuous signs of the disturbance persist for at least 6 months including at least 1 month of active-phase symptoms

Criterion B requires that for a significant portion of time since onset, level of functioning in one or more major areas such as work, interpersonal relations, or self-care is markedly below the level achieved prior to onset
Criterion B requires that schizoaffective disorder and depressive or bipolar disorder with psychotic features have been ruled out
Criterion B requires that the disturbance is not attributable to the physiological effects of a substance or another medical condition

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q15): When the onset of schizophrenia is in childhood or adolescence, DSM-5 Criterion B is modified. What is the modification?
options: There is failure to achieve expected weight gain and physical developmental milestones in childhood or adolescence
There is failure to achieve expected level of interpersonal, academic, or occupational functioning
There is failure to achieve expected social bonding and attachment patterns in childhood or adolescence
There is failure to achieve expected language and communication development in childhood or adolescence

stem: DSM-5 Criterion C specifies the duration of continuous signs. Which statement is MOST completely and precisely stated?
options: Continuous signs of disturbance persist for at least 6 months; this 6-month period must include at least 2 months of active-phase symptoms and may include prodromal or residual periods

Continuous signs of disturbance persist for at least 6 months; this 6-month period must include at least 1 month of active-phase symptoms and may include prodromal or residual periods manifested by negative symptoms or attenuated Criterion A symptoms
Continuous signs of disturbance persist for at least 3 months; this period must include at least 1 month of active-phase symptoms and the remainder may be prodromal or residual
Continuous signs of disturbance persist for at least 6 months; this entire period must consist of active-phase symptoms without any prodromal or residual periods being counted

stem: CASE STUDY: A patient has schizophrenia with concurrent depressive episodes. The clinician must assess whether DSM-5 Criterion D is met. What does Criterion D specifically require?
options: Criterion D requires that intellectual disability and autism spectrum disorder have been ruled out before diagnosing schizophrenia

Criterion D requires that schizoaffective disorder and depressive or bipolar disorder with psychotic features have been ruled out; this means either no major depressive or manic episodes occurred concurrently with active-phase symptoms, or if mood episodes occurred, they were present for a minority of the total duration of the active and residual periods
Criterion D requires that the disturbance is not attributable to the physiological effects of a substance, medication, or general medical condition
Criterion D requires that there is no history of autism spectrum disorder or childhood-onset communication disorder unless prominent delusions or hallucinations are also present

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q18): The psychiatrist explains DSM-5 Criterion F to the patient's family. Which statement MOST precisely reflects Criterion F?
options: If there is a history of autism spectrum disorder or a communication disorder of childhood onset, the additional diagnosis of schizophrenia is made only if prominent delusions or hallucinations â in addition to the other required symptoms â are present for at least 1 month

If there is a history of autism spectrum disorder, schizophrenia cannot be diagnosed regardless of the presence of delusions or hallucinations because symptom overlap makes differential diagnosis impossible

If there is a history of communication disorder of childhood onset, the additional diagnosis of schizophrenia is made only if disorganised speech and negative symptoms are present for at least 6 months
If there is a history of autism spectrum disorder, the diagnosis of schizophrenia is made only if the symptoms began after age 18 and cannot be explained by the developmental trajectory of autism

stem: The notes provide a specific distinction between positive and negative symptoms. Which statement MOST precisely captures this distinction?
options: Positive symptoms are psychotic symptoms which reflect an excess or distortion of normal functions; negative symptoms reflect loss of already acquired normal behaviours which tend to affect normal function

Positive symptoms are favourable signs that indicate good prognosis and include insight, treatment compliance, and social functioning; negative symptoms are unfavourable signs including hallucinations and delusions
Positive symptoms are those present on mental state examination; negative symptoms are those absent on examination â the distinction is based on whether the symptom can be directly observed
Positive symptoms are caused by dopamine excess in the mesolimbic pathway; negative symptoms are caused by dopamine deficiency in the prefrontal cortex â the distinction is purely neurochemical

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse educator asks students to explain the residual phase. Which statement is MOST precisely and completely derived from the notes?
options: The residual phase occurs before the active psychotic phase and is characterised by subclinical psychotic symptoms that do not yet meet full DSM-5 Criterion A threshold

The residual phase usually follows the active phase; symptoms of the acute stage are either absent or no longer prominent; negative symptoms may remain; flat affect and impairment in role functioning are common; residual impairment often increases between episodes
The residual phase is equivalent to the prodromal phase in symptom profile; both are characterised by non-specific symptoms such as anxiety and sleep disturbance without active psychosis
The residual phase occurs only after the first psychotic episode and does not recur after subsequent episodes; subsequent episodes transition directly from active phase to full remission

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q21): A student asks about remission patterns in schizophrenia. Which statement from the notes MOST precisely addresses this?
options: Schizophrenia is characterised by periods of remission and exacerbation; a complete return to full pre-morbid functioning is not common
Schizophrenia typically follows a progressive downhill course without any periods of remission; each acute episode leaves the patient at a lower baseline level of functioning
Schizophrenia is characterised by complete remission after each episode with full return to pre-morbid functioning in most cases; residual impairment is uncommon with adequate treatment
Schizophrenia is characterised by a single acute episode followed by complete recovery in most patients; recurrence is rare if antipsychotic medication is maintained

stem: Several factors are listed as associated with a MORE positive prognosis in schizophrenia. Which list is MOST completely and precisely stated from the notes?
options: Good pre-morbid functioning, later age of onset, female gender, abrupt onset with obvious precipitating factor, associated mood disturbance, rapid resolution of active-phase symptoms, minimal residual symptoms, absence of structural brain abnormalities, normal neurological functioning, and no family history
Good pre-morbid functioning, male gender, early age of onset, insidious onset, absence of mood disturbance, slow resolution of symptoms, and presence of a family history of mood disorder
Good pre-morbid functioning, later age of onset, male gender, insidious onset, associated mood disturbance, rapid resolution of symptoms, and minimal residual symptoms
Good pre-morbid functioning, later age of onset, female gender, gradual onset, absence of mood disturbance, minimal residual symptoms, and presence of structural brain abnormalities

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse asks which type of onset is associated with BETTER prognosis â abrupt or insidious â and why the notes make this distinction.
options: Insidious onset is associated with better prognosis because gradual onset allows the patient and family more time to recognise symptoms and seek treatment before full psychosis develops
Abrupt onset of symptoms with obvious precipitating factor is associated with better prognosis, as opposed to gradual insidious onset of symptoms
Both onset types have equivalent prognosis; the speed of onset affects treatment-seeking behaviour but has no independent prognostic significance once treatment is initiated
Abrupt onset is associated with worse prognosis because it indicates more severe neurological disruption; gradual insidious onset indicates a milder underlying vulnerability

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q24): A student asks about the role of gender in schizophrenia prognosis. Based on the notes, which gender has a better prognosis?
options: Male gender is associated with better prognosis due to higher testosterone levels that are neuroprotective
Female gender is associated with a more positive outcome in schizophrenia
Both genders have equivalent prognosis when controlled for age of onset and symptom severity
Male gender has better prognosis due to greater social independence and occupational opportunities

stem: The lifetime risk for developing schizophrenia is about 1 percent in most population studies. What is the risk for siblings of an identified client?
options: 5 percent risk for siblings
10 percent risk for siblings
15 percent risk for siblings
20 percent risk for siblings

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse counsels a patient whose parent has schizophrenia. What is the offspring's risk of developing schizophrenia when one parent is affected?
options: 1 to 2 percent chance â same as general population risk
5 to 6 percent chance of developing the disorder
10 to 15 percent chance of developing the disorder
20 to 25 percent chance of developing the disorder

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q27): The patient also has an identical twin. Based on the twin study data in the notes, which statement is MOST precisely correct?
options: The rate of schizophrenia among monozygotic twins is four times that of dizygotic twins and approximately 50 times that of the general population; identical twins reared apart have the same rate of illness development as those reared together

The rate of schizophrenia among monozygotic twins is ten times that of dizygotic twins and approximately 100 times that of the general population; identical twins reared apart have a lower rate than those reared together

The rate of schizophrenia among monozygotic twins is two times that of dizygotic twins and approximately 25 times that of the general population; identical twins reared apart have a higher rate than those reared together

The rate of schizophrenia among monozygotic twins is four times that of dizygotic twins and approximately 25 times that of the general population; identical twins reared apart have a lower rate than those reared together

stem: Because in about half of monozygotic twin pairs only one twin develops schizophrenia, what conclusion do some investigators draw?
options: That schizophrenia is entirely genetic in origin and the unaffected twin represents a phenotypic variant with subclinical schizophrenia spectrum symptoms
That environmental factors interact with genetic ones in the aetiology of schizophrenia
That the diagnostic criteria for schizophrenia are too broad and include phenotypically distinct disorders with different genetic loadings
That epigenetic silencing of schizophrenia-related genes in the unaffected twin prevents phenotypic expression of the inherited vulnerability

stem: CASE STUDY: A student states that schizophrenia has a single, identifiable cause. Based on the predisposing factors section of the notes, which rebuttal is MOST precise?
options: The cause is confirmed to be entirely genetic; neuroimaging studies have identified the specific gene loci responsible for all cases of schizophrenia
Most likely no single factor can be implicated in the aetiology; rather, the disease probably results from a combination of biological, psychological, and environmental factors
The cause is confirmed to be entirely neurobiological; dopamine dysregulation has been established as the sole mechanism explaining all positive and negative symptoms
The cause is confirmed to be entirely environmental; childhood trauma and adverse life events are the primary aetiological factors identified in all population-based studies

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q30): A colleague states: 'Since schizophrenia has no single cause, it cannot be studied scientifically.' The most accurate rebuttal from the notes is:
options: This is incorrect; because schizophrenia's causes are an unsolved puzzle, it has probably been studied more than any other mental disorder
This is incorrect; the DSM-5 provides a single definitive biological biomarker for schizophrenia that enables objective scientific measurement
This is correct; the absence of a single cause means schizophrenia cannot be operationally defined for research purposes
This is incorrect; schizophrenia research is limited to genetic studies because only genetic factors are amenable to controlled scientific investigation

stem: DSM-5 Criterion E for schizophrenia requires exclusion of a specific class of causes. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: The disturbance is not attributable to cultural or religious practices, spiritual experiences, or normative responses to bereavement
The disturbance is not attributable to the physiological effects of a substance such as a drug of abuse or a medication, or another medical condition
The disturbance is not better explained by a neurodevelopmental disorder including intellectual disability or autism spectrum disorder
The disturbance is not attributable to psychosocial stressors or adverse life events that commonly trigger brief psychotic episodes

stem: CASE STUDY: A psychiatric nurse notes that during the residual phase, residual impairment increases over time. What specific pattern does the text describe?
options: Residual impairment increases between episodes of active psychosis, creating a stepwise decline in functioning with each recurrence
Residual impairment decreases between episodes as the brain adapts to repeated dopaminergic dysregulation
Residual impairment remains stable between episodes and only increases during active psychotic phases
Residual impairment is reversible with consistent antipsychotic medication and shows no long-term progressive pattern

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q33): During the residual phase, which specific symptoms remain prominent according to the notes?
options: Auditory hallucinations and paranoid delusions remain prominent; positive symptoms persist at a subclinical level throughout the residual phase
Negative symptoms may remain, and flat affect and impairment in role functioning are common during the residual phase
Disorganised speech and grossly disorganised behaviour remain prominent; these Criterion A symptoms persist in attenuated form throughout the residual phase
Anxiety, sleep disturbance, and poor concentration remain prominent; these non-specific symptoms are indistinguishable from the prodromal phase

stem: The notes specify that in the prodromal or residual periods, the signs of disturbance may be manifested in specific ways. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Only by negative symptoms or by two or more Criterion A symptoms present in an attenuated form such as odd beliefs and unusual perceptual experiences
Only by positive symptoms such as hallucinations and delusions in attenuated form; negative symptoms do not appear in prodromal or residual periods
Only by disorganised speech and behaviour; mood and anxiety symptoms are not considered part of schizophrenia prodrome
Only by one Criterion A symptom present in attenuated form; at least one active Criterion A symptom must also be present simultaneously

stem: CASE STUDY: A third-year nursing student is asked about the prognosis of schizophrenia. Which statement is MOST precisely derived from the notes?
options: Outcomes in schizophrenia are difficult to predict, but a complete return to full pre-morbid functioning is not common; however, several factors have been associated with a more positive outcome
Outcomes in schizophrenia are well-established and predictable; complete remission is achieved in approximately 80 percent of patients who receive adequate antipsychotic medication
Outcomes in schizophrenia are uniformly poor regardless of treatment; no factors have been reliably associated with a more positive outcome in longitudinal studies
Outcomes in schizophrenia are positive for the majority; most patients achieve full pre-morbid functioning with modern antipsychotic medication and psychosocial rehabilitation

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q36): A nurse asks whether two factors associated with WORSE prognosis can be identified from the good prognosis list by inversion. Which combination MOST accurately reflects the logical inverse of the good prognosis factors?
options: Poor pre-morbid functioning and early age of onset are associated with worse prognosis â the inverse of good pre-morbid functioning and later age of onset
Male gender and insidious onset are associated with better prognosis â male gender confers protection similar to oestrogen's neuroprotective role in females
Presence of structural brain abnormalities is associated with better prognosis â abnormal neurodevelopment triggers adaptive neuroplasticity
Early age of onset and associated mood disturbance are both associated with worse prognosis â mood disturbance destabilises the therapeutic relationship

stem: The disorganised speech described as a DSM-5 Criterion A symptom is exemplified by which specific features?
options: Mutism, echolalia, and stereotyped verbal repetition
Frequent derailment or incoherence
Pressured speech, flight of ideas, and tangentiality
Poverty of speech, alogia, and long latency of responses

stem: CASE STUDY: A 28-year-old man has a 7-month history of auditory hallucinations and paranoid delusions. He has been isolating at home, lost his job, and stopped caring for himself. His urine toxicology is negative and his MRI is normal. There is no history of autism or communication disorders. DSM-5 criteria are being evaluated. Which criteria combination is MET?
options: Only Criteria A and B are met; Criterion C is not met because 7 months does not include 2 months of active-phase symptoms
Criteria A, B, C, D, and E are all met; the duration exceeds 6 months with active symptoms, functioning is impaired, mood disorders and substance use are excluded
Only Criteria A and E are met; Criterion B is not met because occupational impairment began before the psychotic symptoms
All criteria are met but Criterion F cannot be assessed without a childhood developmental history

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q39): The attending psychiatrist specifies the course of illness. Which specifiers does DSM-5 allow?
options: First episode or multiple episodes only; no other specifiers are provided
First episode currently in acute, partial, or full remission; multiple episodes currently in acute, partial, or full remission; continuous; unspecified; and with or without catatonia
First episode or relapsing-remitting course only; severity specifiers are not included in DSM-5
First episode currently in acute phase; multiple episodes currently in acute phase; in remission; and treatment-resistant

stem: Which statement MOST precisely explains WHY twin studies â specifically the fact that identical twins reared apart have the same rate of schizophrenia as those reared together â are significant for understanding schizophrenia aetiology?
options: Because it confirms that shared environmental factors such as family stress and common household exposures are the primary cause of schizophrenia in families

Because it demonstrates that even without shared environmental influences, genetic vulnerability expresses similarly â strongly supporting a genetic contribution to schizophrenia that is independent of rearing environment
Because it proves that schizophrenia is entirely genetic with no environmental component, since rearing environment makes no difference to the rate of illness development
Because it shows that adoption away from a schizophrenic parent is not protective, confirming that the parenting behaviour of the affected parent â rather than genetics â transmits the disorder

stem: CASE STUDY: A community nurse is meeting a family whose daughter has recently received a diagnosis of schizophrenia. The mother asks why the disorder developed despite no family history. Which explanation is MOST grounded in the notes?
options: Schizophrenia without family history is caused exclusively by environmental factors such as prenatal infection or urban birth environment

The cause is still uncertain; most likely no single factor can be implicated in the aetiology â it probably results from a combination of biological, psychological, and environmental factors, and absence of family history does not exclude genetic vulnerability
Without family history, schizophrenia is always caused by substance use or a general medical condition; Criterion E must be reassessed before the diagnosis is confirmed
Absence of family history confirms that the case is purely environmental in origin; genetic counselling is not indicated for other family members

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q42): The nurse explains the three broad predisposing factor categories for schizophrenia. Which combination is MOST precisely stated?
options: Neurological, pharmacological, and social factors
Biological, psychological, and environmental factors
Genetic, neurochemical, and cultural factors
Biological, developmental, and societal factors

stem: The notes state that negative symptoms in the DSM-5 specifically include which two examples?
options: Anhedonia and asociality
Diminished emotional expression or avolition
Flat affect and alogia
Social withdrawal and poverty of thought

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks why a good pre-morbid functioning is listed as a positive prognostic factor in schizophrenia. Which explanation is MOST consistent with the clinical reasoning in the notes?
options: Good pre-morbid functioning suggests a milder genetic loading and therefore a less severe neurobiological substrate for the illness

Good pre-morbid functioning indicates the person had stronger psychological and social resources before illness onset, which are associated with better outcomes and are part of the profile of factors predicting a more positive prognosis
Good pre-morbid functioning is a marker for later age of onset, which is the actual causal prognostic factor â pre-morbid functioning has no independent prognostic value
Good pre-morbid functioning ensures better medication adherence, which is the mechanistic pathway through which it improves prognosis in schizophrenia

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q45): The student asks whether minimal residual symptoms are associated with better or worse prognosis. The correct answer based on the notes is:
options: Minimal residual symptoms are associated with worse prognosis because they indicate the patient is undermedicated and at high risk of rapid relapse
Minimal residual symptoms are associated with better prognosis â they are explicitly listed as one of the factors associated with a more positive outcome
Minimal residual symptoms have no independent prognostic value; prognosis is determined entirely by the severity of the acute phase
Minimal residual symptoms are associated with worse prognosis because they mask ongoing neurobiological deterioration that is not clinically apparent

stem: Which structural and neurological prognostic factors are specifically listed in the notes as associated with better outcome?
options: Enlarged ventricles, hypofrontal activity, and family history of bipolar disorder
Absence of structural brain abnormalities, normal neurological functioning, and no family history of schizophrenia
Normal hippocampal volume, preserved frontal lobe metabolism, and family history of good treatment response
Absence of brain lesions, normal EEG, and normal cognitive testing at baseline

stem: The term schizophrenia was coined in 1908 by the Swiss psychiatrist _______________ Bleuler.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'The word schizophrenia was derived from the Greek skhizo meaning split and phren meaning _______________.' One precise noun must be supplied.
About one-third of people with schizophrenia attempt suicide and about 1 in _______________ die from the act.

CASE STUDY: An exam question reads: 'The pattern of development of schizophrenia may be viewed in four phases: the pre-morbid phase, the _______________ phase, the active psychotic phase, and the residual phase.' One phase name must be supplied.
The average length of the prodromal phase is between _______________ and 5 years.

CASE STUDY: A student is asked: 'In the pre-morbid history of schizophrenia, patients usually have schizoid or schizotypal personalities characterised as quiet, _______________, and introverted.' One adjective must be supplied.
Under DSM-5 Criterion A, two or more symptoms must each be present for a significant portion of time during a _______________-month period.
CASE STUDY: An examiner states: 'Under DSM-5 Criterion A, at least one symptom must be (1) delusions, (2) hallucinations, or (3) _______________.' One precise symptom must be supplied.
Criterion C requires that continuous signs of disturbance persist for at least _______________ months, including at least 1 month of active-phase symptoms.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'Positive symptoms are psychotic symptoms which tend to reflect an excess or _______________ of normal functions.' One precise noun must be supplied.
Negative symptoms reflect loss of already acquired normal behaviours which tend to affect the _______________ function.

CASE STUDY: A student is told: 'The rate of schizophrenia among monozygotic twins is four times that of dizygotic twins and approximately _______________ times that of the general population.' One precise number must be supplied.
The siblings of an identified client with schizophrenia have a _______________ percent risk of developing schizophrenia.
CASE STUDY: An exam asks: 'Offspring with one parent who has schizophrenia have a _______________ to 6 percent chance of developing the disorder.' One precise number must be supplied.
The residual phase usually follows the _______________ phase of the illness.
CASE STUDY: A student is asked: 'Residual impairment often _______________ between episodes of active psychosis.' One precise verb from the notes must be supplied.
A complete return to full _______________ functioning is not common in schizophrenia, even with treatment.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'Among the factors associated with more positive outcome in schizophrenia, _______________ gender is specifically listed.' One word must be supplied.
The fifth DSM-5 Criterion A symptom is negative symptoms defined as diminished emotional expression or _______________.

CASE STUDY: A student is asked to complete this statement from the notes: 'Because in about half of the cases only one of a pair of monozygotic twins develops schizophrenia, some investigators believe _______________ factors interact with genetic ones.' One category must be supplied.

In Criterion D, mood episodes that occurred during active-phase symptoms must have been present for a _______________ of the total duration of active and residual periods to NOT rule out schizophrenia.

CASE STUDY: An examiner states: 'Schizophrenia is probably the most devastating illness that psychiatrists treat; it strikes people typically in the _______________ or early 20s.' One period of life must be supplied.

Under DSM-5 Criterion F, when there is a history of autism spectrum disorder or childhood-onset communication disorder, the additional diagnosis of schizophrenia is made only if prominent delusions or hallucinations are present for at least _______________ month.
